title: Automatic Film Processing
narrationScript: An automatic processor does exactly four things to your film, the same four things manual processing does. It develops, fixes, washes, and dries. But it does them the same way every time, and that consistency is the whole point. This lesson is about how the machine works, how to feed it, and how to keep it honest. Follow the film through. You set a sheet on the feed tray and the entrance rollers take it. From that moment the film rides a chain of soft rollers arranged in deep racks, down into the developer tank, around the bottom turnaround, back up, across a crossover into the fixer, through the wash rack, then between squeegee rollers that strip the surface water, and finally into a dryer where heated, filtered air finishes the job. There is no stop bath in most machines. The squeegee action of the rollers and the acid fixer itself arrest development. A typical industrial unit hands you a dry, readable radiograph in around eight to twelve minutes door to door, and faster machines exist. Notice what disappeared. Nobody chose a development time. In an automatic processor, development time is set by the transport speed and the length of the developer rack, so the only way the time changes is if the machine speed changes. Temperature is handled the same way, by a thermostat, a heat exchanger, and constant recirculation that also filters the developer. The chemistry runs hot compared to a manual tank, typically near eighty degrees Fahrenheit in industrial machines, which is why processor chemistry includes hardeners and a starter formulated so the emulsion can survive hot solutions and roller contact. Manual and processor chemicals are not interchangeable. Replenishment is automatic too, and it is smarter than a bucket. A detector at the entrance senses film going in, and replenisher pumps run while the film feeds, metering fresh developer and fixer in proportion to the area of film processed. That is why feeding habits matter. Feed films squarely, alternate sides of the tray so the rollers and the chemistry wear evenly, keep a consistent orientation so the area metering stays true, and wait for the machine's signal before feeding the next sheet, because two films overlapping in the rollers become one expensive jam. Never yank a film back out against the rollers. Now, keeping the machine honest. A processor is consistent at whatever state it happens to be in, including a bad state, and it will repeat a fault on every film until somebody notices. So we give it a physical every morning. Check solution levels and temperatures, make sure the water is flowing, run a cleanup film to pick up dirt from the rollers, and then process a sensitometric control strip, a film given a precise, repeatable exposure by a calibrated sensitometer. Read the strip on a calibrated densitometer and plot three numbers on a control chart. A mid density step that tracks speed, a density difference that tracks contrast, and base plus fog. Typical programs act when speed or contrast moves about fifteen hundredths of density from aim, or when fog climbs about five hundredths. If a point lands outside the action limits, production films wait until you find the cause, fix it, and rerun a strip that comes back inside. Maintenance is the other half. Daily, wipe the crossover rollers and the feed rollers, because dried chemistry on a crossover prints marks on every film that follows. Weekly, or as the manual directs, pull the racks and clean them properly. And learn the machine's signature artifacts. Pi lines are fine lines across the film that repeat at a spacing of about three point one four times the roller diameter, once per roller revolution, and they point to a dirty or crusted roller. Pick off marks are tiny spots where a sticky roller lifted emulsion. Chatter and hesitation bands come from drive problems. Films that emerge damp or tacky say the dryer is weak or the fixer hardener is exhausted. Scratches in neat straight lines usually trace to a misadjusted guide shoe. So the trade is clear. The processor buys you speed, throughput, and films that match from morning to night, and in exchange it demands clean rollers, fresh balanced chemistry, a daily control strip, and an operator who treats it like the precision instrument it is. Feed it carefully, chart it daily, clean it on schedule, and it will hand you code quality radiographs all shift long.
imagePrompt: A technician feeds a sheet of radiographic film into the feed tray of a benchtop automatic film processor with its lid raised to reveal roller racks above the chemical tanks in a clean, brightly lit processing room.

sections.heading: From Tanks to Rollers
sections.body:
An automatic processor performs the same chemistry as the manual line - develop, fix, wash, dry - but locks every variable a human hand used to control. Film feeds between entrance rollers, rides roller racks down and up through the **developer**, across a crossover into the **fixer**, through the **wash**, between **squeegee rollers**, and into a **dryer** of heated, filtered air. Most machines have no stop bath; the squeegee action of the rollers and the acid fixer arrest development. A typical industrial unit delivers a dry radiograph in about **8-12 minutes dry-to-dry**; high-speed machines are faster still.

The defining fact: **development time is set by the transport speed** and the length of the developer rack. Nobody chooses a time, and the only way time drifts is a drive problem. Temperature is thermostatic, held by a heat exchanger with constant **recirculation** that also filters the developer. Processor chemistry runs hot - near **80 °F** in typical industrial machines, hotter in high-speed designs - and is formulated with hardeners and starter so the emulsion survives hot solutions and roller contact. Manual and processor chemistries are **not interchangeable**.

sections.caption: Cutaway of a roller-transport processor: feed tray and entrance detector, developer, fixer, and wash racks, squeegee rollers, dryer, with metered replenishment and recirculated, thermostatically controlled chemistry.

sections.heading: Replenishment and Feeding Discipline
sections.body:
Replenishment is metered, not guessed. A **detector at the feed tray** senses film entering and runs the replenisher pumps while the film feeds, adding fresh developer and fixer from storage tanks in proportion to the **area of film processed**. Starved replenishment shows up as falling speed and contrast on the QC chart; over-replenishment wastes chemistry and can push fog upward.

Because the machine meters by what passes the detector, feeding habits matter:

- Feed films **squarely**, never skewed.
- **Alternate sides** of the feed tray so rollers and chemistry work evenly across their width.
- Keep a **consistent orientation** so area metering matches the rates set at installation.
- **Wait for the feed signal** before the next sheet - overlapped films jam racks and ruin both sheets.
- Never pull a film back against the rollers; if the machine takes one badly, let it pass or shut down and open the racks.
- Pieces too small to operate the detector reliably are handled per the manual, not improvised.

sections.heading: Daily Processor Quality Control
sections.body:
A processor is consistent at whatever state it is in - including a bad one - so it gets a physical every morning before production film. Check levels, temperatures, and water flow; run a **cleanup film** to collect roller dirt; then process a **sensitometric control strip**: film given a precise, repeatable exposure by a calibrated **sensitometer**.

Read the strip on a **densitometer** (verified against a traceable calibrated step tablet) and plot three values on a control chart:

- a **mid-density (speed) step**,
- a **density difference (contrast index)** between two chosen steps,
- **base-plus-fog**.

Typical programs set action limits near **plus or minus 0.15 density** for speed and contrast and about **plus 0.05** for fog. Inside the limits, run production. Outside, stop: find the cause - temperature, replenishment, contaminated or mismixed chemistry, transport speed - correct it, and rerun a strip that lands inside before any production film moves. Re-baseline whenever the QC film emulsion lot or the chemistry type changes. The chart is also early-warning radar: a steady drift toward a limit is a problem announcing itself in advance.

sections.caption: Daily sensitometric control chart: speed-step density plotted against aim with action limits, showing a drift that ends in an out-of-limit point requiring correction before production resumes.

sections.heading: Maintenance and Processor Artifacts
sections.body:
Cleaning is image quality. **Daily**: wipe the crossover and entrance rollers - dried chemistry on a crossover prints on every film that follows - and verify dryer heat and smooth startup. **Weekly or per the manual**: pull the racks, clean rollers and guide shoes properly, inspect springs and gears. **On schedule**: drain, clean, and refill tanks, service recirculation filters, and control algae in the wash.

The machine's signature artifacts identify their own causes:

- **Pi lines** - fine lines across the film repeating every **3.14 times the roller diameter** (once per roller revolution): deposits or a flat spot on a roller.
- **Pick-off marks** - tiny spots of lifted emulsion from sticky or rough rollers.
- **Chatter or hesitation bands** - uneven density bars from drive irregularities.
- **Straight-line scratches** - misadjusted guide shoes scoring the soft emulsion.
- **Damp, tacky films** - weak dryer, exhausted fixer hardener, or unbalanced replenishment.
- **Overlap marks and jams** - films fed too soon or skewed.

Every one of these is cheaper to fix at the morning cleanup than to discover across a day's production radiographs - and a processor artifact identified on the film is an artifact, never a rejectable indication.

sections.heading: What the Machine Buys You - and What It Costs
sections.body:
Against the manual line of the previous lesson, the processor's advantages are decisive for production work: **dry-to-dry in minutes instead of an hour**, hands-off consistency that holds density and contrast steady across shifts and operators, throughput that keeps up with a busy exposure crew, and replenishment metered to actual workload instead of judgment. For code radiography, that consistency is the quiet hero - exposure techniques are built on the assumption that today's processing matches the day the technique chart was qualified.

The costs are real and worth respecting. Capital, installation, water, drains, and power. Chemistry formulated for hot roller transport, bought and mixed correctly. Scheduled cleaning that cannot slip, because roller deposits print on film. A daily QC habit, because the operator no longer feels the process through a hanger and a timer. And a failure mode manual lines do not have: a transport jam or a sick chemistry state can damage or mismark a whole batch before anyone intervenes. Small shops with low film volume, odd film sizes, or field darkrooms often keep a manual line for exactly these reasons - the right answer is workload-dependent, not fashion-dependent.

sections.title: Automatic does not mean unattended
sections.body: The processor's consistency is a multiplier, not a guarantee: it repeats whatever state it is in onto every film of the shift. Daily control strips read against action limits, clean crossovers, metered replenishment, and an operator who actually reads the chart are what turn 'automatic' into 'reliable'.

checkQuestions.question: In an automatic processor, development time is determined by:
options: The operator's setting on a development timer
The transport speed and the length of the developer rack
The replenishment rate of the developer
The temperature of the dryer section
checkQuestions.explanation: The film spends exactly as long in the developer as the rollers take to carry it through the rack, so transport speed - not an operator timer - fixes development time.

checkQuestions.question: Automatic replenishment of developer and fixer is triggered by:
options: A timer that adds chemistry every hour of operation
The operator pressing the replenish button after each batch
A detector at the feed tray that runs the pumps in proportion to the film area entering
A float valve that tops up the tanks with water
checkQuestions.explanation: The entrance detector senses film feeding in and meters replenisher to match the area processed - which is also why films must be fed squarely and consistently.

checkQuestions.question: Fine lines across a processed film repeat at a spacing of about 3.14 times the roller diameter. These are:
options: Pi lines, from deposits on a roller printing once per revolution
Static marks from sliding the film into the feed tray
Crimp marks from bending the film
Backscatter images from missing backing lead
checkQuestions.explanation: One full roller revolution covers the circumference, pi times the diameter, so a dirty or flatted roller marks the film at exactly that repeat distance - the classic pi line.

checkQuestions.question: The morning quality-control routine that qualifies a processor for production work is:
options: Processing one production radiograph and judging it by eye
Running a sensitometric control strip and plotting speed, contrast, and fog against control-chart limits
Measuring the developer temperature with any available thermometer
Wiping the crossover rollers and feeding film immediately
checkQuestions.explanation: Only a repeatable sensitometric exposure read on a calibrated densitometer and charted against action limits proves the whole chemical-mechanical system is where it was yesterday.

checkQuestions.question: Films are emerging from the processor damp and tacky. The two prime suspects are:
options: Excess developer replenishment and a fast transport speed
A weak or under-heated dryer and exhausted fixer hardener
An over-active stop bath and cold wash water
Too much backing lead and a thick front screen
checkQuestions.explanation: Drying depends on heated airflow and on emulsion hardened by the fixer; a weak dryer or spent hardener leaves films wet and easily damaged. Most processors have no stop bath at all.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality 2.5 (automatic film processing)
ASTM E999-style guidance on controlling radiographic film processing quality (reference practice)
Processor manufacturer operating and maintenance manuals (reference practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (radiographic examination, general practice)
